data.content:
### Download PostHog CLI

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/docs/error-tracking/upload-source-maps/cli.mdx

This snippet provides instructions on how to download the PostHog CLI. It is a prerequisite for uploading source maps and other CLI operations. Ensure you have the correct download command for your operating system.

```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Example for Linux/macOS
curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/PostHog/posthog-cli/main/get-posthog.sh | /bin/bash

# Example for Windows (PowerShell)
# Invoke-WebRequest -Uri "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/PostHog/posthog-cli/main/get-posthog.sh" -OutFile "get-posthog.sh"
# bash get-posthog.sh

```

--------------------------------

### Download PostHog CLI

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/docs/error-tracking/upload-source-maps/node.mdx

Installs the PostHog Command Line Interface (CLI), which is necessary for uploading source maps and other project-related tasks.

```bash
#!/bin/bash

# This is a placeholder for the actual CLI download command.
# Refer to PostHog documentation for the correct command.

echo "Downloading PostHog CLI..."
# Example: curl -L https://github.com/posthog/posthog-cli/releases/latest/download/posthog-cli_$(uname -s)_$(uname -m).tar.gz | tar xvz
# Example: ./posthog version
```

--------------------------------

### Authenticate PostHog CLI with Login Command

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/docs/error-tracking/_snippets/cli/authenticate.mdx

This command initiates an interactive login process for the PostHog CLI. It guides the user through steps to authenticate their CLI session. No specific inputs are required, but user interaction is necessary.

```bash
posthog-cli login
```

--------------------------------

### Authenticate PostHog CLI with Environment Variables (CI/CD)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/docs/error-tracking/_snippets/cli/authenticate.mdx

This method allows for non-interactive authentication of the PostHog CLI, suitable for CI/CD pipelines. It requires setting two environment variables: `POSTHOG_CLI_ENV_ID` for the project ID and `POSTHOG_CLI_TOKEN` for a personal API key with the necessary scope. Ensure these variables are securely managed.

```bash
# Example of setting environment variables (syntax may vary by shell)
export POSTHOG_CLI_ENV_ID="your_project_id"
export POSTHOG_CLI_TOKEN="your_api_token"
```

--------------------------------

### Authenticate PostHog CLI

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/docs/error-tracking/upload-source-maps/cli.mdx

This snippet shows how to authenticate the PostHog CLI with your PostHog project. Authentication is required to authorize the CLI to upload data to your PostHog instance. You will need your PostHog API key.

```bash
# Replace YOUR_API_KEY and YOUR_POSTHOG_INSTANCE with your actual values
posthog --api-key YOUR_API_KEY --host YOUR_POSTHOG_INSTANCE auth login

```

--------------------------------

### Specify PostHog Host for CLI Commands

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/docs/error-tracking/_snippets/cli/authenticate.mdx

This command demonstrates how to specify a custom PostHog instance host for CLI operations. This is useful for users in different regions, such as the EU, or when using a self-hosted PostHog instance. Replace `[CMD]` with the actual PostHog CLI command you wish to execute.

```bash
posthog-cli --host https://eu.posthog.com [CMD]
```

--------------------------------

### Install PostHog CLI for Source Map Upload

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/docs/error-tracking/upload-source-maps/web.mdx

Provides the command to download and install the PostHog CLI, a prerequisite for uploading source maps. This tool is essential for interacting with the PostHog API for source map management.

```bash
#!/bin/bash
curl -sL https://posthog.com/install.sh | bash

```

--------------------------------

### PostHog CLI: Download and Authenticate

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/docs/error-tracking/upload-source-maps/angular.mdx

These snippets demonstrate how to download and authenticate the PostHog CLI. The CLI is essential for uploading source maps and other artifacts to PostHog. Ensure you have a valid API key for authentication.

```bash
# Example download command (replace with actual command if provided elsewhere)
# posthog cli download

# Example authentication command
posthog auth login --token "YOUR_POSTHOG_API_KEY"
```

--------------------------------

### Authenticate PostHog CLI

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/docs/error-tracking/upload-source-maps/node.mdx

Authenticates the PostHog CLI with your PostHog project using an API key. This allows the CLI to securely interact with your PostHog instance.

```bash
#!/bin/bash

# This is a placeholder for the actual CLI authentication command.
# Replace YOUR_API_KEY and YOUR_POSTHOG_HOST with your actual values.

export POSTHOG_TOKEN="YOUR_API_KEY"
export POSTHOG_HOST="YOUR_POSTHOG_HOST"

# Example: ./posthog login --token $POSTHOG_TOKEN --host $POSTHOG_HOST

echo "Authenticating PostHog CLI..."

```

--------------------------------

### Upload Source Maps with CLI

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/docs/error-tracking/upload-source-maps/cli.mdx

This snippet shows the command to upload your source maps to PostHog after injecting the necessary metadata. Ensure the `--path` points to the directory containing your source maps and bundled assets. This allows PostHog to de-minify stack traces effectively.

```bash
# Replace ./path/to/assets with the directory containing your bundled assets and source maps
posthog-cli sourcemap upload ./path/to/assets

```

--------------------------------

### Install PostgreSQL CLI Tools on macOS

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/handbook/engineering/developing-locally.md

Installs PostgreSQL server and its command-line tools on macOS using Homebrew. This is needed for its CLI tools and development libraries required by 'psycopg2'. Do not start the server after installation.

```bash
brew install postgresql
```

--------------------------------

### Inject Source Map Metadata with CLI

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/docs/error-tracking/upload-source-maps/cli.mdx

This snippet demonstrates how to inject metadata into your bundled JavaScript assets using the PostHog CLI. This step is crucial for associating source maps with your deployed code, enabling accurate debugging of minified and obfuscated code. The `--chunk-id` flag should match your build process.

```bash
# Replace ./path/to/your/dist with the actual path to your bundled assets
# The --chunk-id flag should match the naming convention of your build tool (e.g., webpack, vite)
posthog-cli sourcemap inject ./path/to/your/dist --chunk-id "[name]" --base-path "/static/js/"

```

--------------------------------

### Authenticate PostHog CLI for Source Map Upload

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/docs/error-tracking/upload-source-maps/web.mdx

Demonstrates how to authenticate the PostHog CLI using an API key. This step is crucial for authorizing the CLI to upload source maps to your PostHog project. Ensure you have your PostHog API key ready.

```bash
posthog auth login --token "YOUR_POSTHOG_API_KEY"

```

--------------------------------

### Create New Remix App CLI

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/tutorials/remix-ab-tests.md

Command to create a new Remix application using the `create-remix` CLI tool. It prompts the user for the application name and default options.

```bash
npx create-remix@latest
```

--------------------------------

### Generate and Upload Nuxt Source Maps with PostHog CLI

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/docs/error-tracking/upload-source-maps/nuxt.mdx

This snippet configures the Nuxt build process to generate sourcemaps and upload them to PostHog using the PostHog CLI. It hooks into the 'close' event of the Nuxt build to execute CLI commands for injecting and uploading sourcemaps. Ensure the PostHog CLI is installed and authenticated prior to building.

```javascript
import { execSync } from 'child_process'

export default defineNuxtConfig({
  ...,
  sourcemap: {
    client: true
  },
  hooks: {
    'close': async () => {
      console.log('Running PostHog sourcemap injection and upload...')
      try {
        execSync("posthog-cli sourcemap inject --directory '.output'", {
          stdio: 'inherit',
        })
        execSync("posthog-cli sourcemap upload --directory '.output'", {
          stdio: 'inherit',
        })
        console.log('PostHog sourcemap injection completed successfully')
      } catch (error) {
        console.error('PostHog sourcemap injection failed:', error)
      }
    }
  }
})
```

--------------------------------

### AWS CLI Session Token with MFA

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/handbook/engineering/cloud-providers.md

Resolves AWS CLI permission errors caused by explicit denies, often due to MFA. This process involves obtaining a session token using the AWS CLI, which requires your MFA device details. The obtained credentials should be used for the duration of your session and unset afterward.

```bash
aws sts get-session-token --serial-number <arn-of-the-mfa-device> --token-code <code-from-token> --duration 129600
```

```bash
export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=example-access-key-as-in-previous-output
export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=example-secret-access-key-as-in-previous-output
export AWS_SESSION_TOKEN=example-session-token-as-in-previous-output
```

```bash
unset AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID && unset AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY && unset AWS_SESSION_TOKEN
```

--------------------------------

### Install Claude Code CLI

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/tutorials/deskhog-claude-tutorial.md

Installs the Anthropic Claude Code command-line interface globally using npm. This tool enables natural language interaction for code generation and modification within a project.

```bash
npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code
```

--------------------------------

### Install SQLx CLI for Database Migrations

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/handbook/engineering/developing-locally.md

Installs the SQLx Command Line Interface, a tool essential for managing database migrations in Rust projects. This command assumes Cargo, the Rust package manager, is already installed.

```shell
cargo install sqlx-cli
```

--------------------------------

### PostHog CLI: Upload Source Maps

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/docs/error-tracking/upload-source-maps/angular.mdx

This command uses the PostHog CLI to upload your generated source maps. Ensure you specify the correct project and version to associate the source maps with your deployed application.

```bash
# Example upload command (replace with actual command if provided elsewhere)
# posthog source-maps upload --project "YOUR_PROJECT_SLUG" --version "YOUR_APP_VERSION" "path/to/your/source/maps"
```

--------------------------------

### Install Vue CLI

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/tutorials/vue-cookie-banner.md

Installs the Vue command line interface globally using npm. This is a prerequisite for creating new Vue projects.

```bash
npm install -g @vue/cli
```

--------------------------------

### Uploading Source Maps with PostHog CLI

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/docs/error-tracking/stack-traces.mdx

This example shows how to use the `posthog-cli` to upload source maps, a crucial step for compiled or minified languages to enable PostHog to display accurate stack traces. This command should be integrated into your build process.

```bash
posthog-cli upload-sourcemaps --api-key YOUR_POSTHOG_API_KEY --source-maps "./dist/**/*.js.map" --bundle "./dist/**/*.js"
```

--------------------------------

### Install and Initialize Angular CLI

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/tutorials/angular-surveys.md

Installs the Angular CLI globally and creates a new Angular project. It requires Node.js to be installed. This sets up the foundational structure for an Angular application.

```bash
npm install -g @angular/cli
ng new angular-surveys
```

--------------------------------

### Create and Navigate Angular App

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/tutorials/angular-analytics.md

Uses the Angular CLI to create a new Angular project with routing and then navigates into the project directory.

```bash
npx -p @angular/cli@16 ng new angular-ph --routing
cd angular-ph
```

--------------------------------

### Create Vue App using Vue CLI

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/tutorials/vue-feature-flags.md

This snippet shows the command-line interface commands to install the Vue CLI and create a new Vue 3 project. It requires Node.js version 18.0 or newer to be installed.

```bash
npm install -g @vue/cli
vue create vue-feature-flags
```

--------------------------------

### Generate Angular Components

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/tutorials/angular-analytics.md

Generates 'home' and 'about' components for the Angular application using the Angular CLI.

```bash
ng generate component home
ng generate component about
```

--------------------------------

### Install PostHog React Native SDK for React Native CLI

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/docs/integrate/_snippets/install-react-native.mdx

Installs the posthog-react-native package and necessary peer dependencies for React Native projects managed via the React Native CLI. Supports both Yarn and npm package managers.

```bash
yarn add posthog-react-native @react-native-async-storage/async-storage react-native-device-info react-native-localize
```

```bash
npm i -s posthog-react-native @react-native-async-storage/async-storage react-native-device-info react-native-localize
```

--------------------------------

### Install Angular CLI and Create New App

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/tutorials/angular-ab-tests.md

Installs the Angular CLI globally and creates a new Angular project. This is the first step in setting up an Angular application for A/B testing.

```bash
npm install -g @angular/cli
ng new angular-ab-tests
```

--------------------------------

### Create Next.js App with Pages Router

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/tutorials/nextjs-pages-analytics.md

This command initializes a new Next.js application using the create-next-app CLI. It specifically configures the project to use the pages router and avoids TypeScript, setting up a basic structure for the application.

```bash
npx create-next-app@latest pages-tutorial
cd pages-tutorial
npm run dev
```

--------------------------------

### Create Vue App CLI

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/tutorials/vue-surveys.md

Installs the Vue CLI globally and creates a new Vue 3 project. Requires Node.js version 18.0 or newer.

```bash
npm install -g @vue/cli
vue create vue-surveys
```

--------------------------------

### Run Database Migrations

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/handbook/engineering/developing-locally.md

Executes database migrations using a local 'migrate' script, potentially with debug logging enabled. Assumes SQLX CLI is installed and configured.

```bash
DEBUG=1 ./bin/migrate
```

--------------------------------

### Install PostHog .NET SDK

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/tutorials/dotnet-analytics.md

Command to add the PostHog .NET SDK package to a project using the .NET CLI. This enables PostHog analytics functionality within the application.

```bash
dotnet add package PostHog.AspNetCore --prerelease
```

--------------------------------

### Upload Source Maps to PostHog

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/docs/error-tracking/upload-source-maps/web.mdx

This command uploads your generated source maps to PostHog. Ensure you have already authenticated the CLI and that your source maps are correctly generated and placed. This enables PostHog to de-minify stack traces from your web application.

```bash
posthog source-maps upload --directory=./dist --project-api-key="YOUR_POSTHOG_PROJECT_API_KEY" --app-url="YOUR_APP_URL"

```

--------------------------------

### Initialize React Native Expo App

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/tutorials/react-native-analytics.md

This command initializes a new React Native project using the Expo CLI. Ensure you have Node.js, Git, and the Expo CLI installed globally. This step creates the basic project structure for your application.

```bash
npx create-expo-app rn-tutorial
cd rn-tutorial
npx expo start
```

--------------------------------

### Create Pull Request via Command Line (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/handbook/engineering/posthog-com/developing-the-website.md

This snippet demonstrates the command-line output when pushing a new branch to GitHub and initiating the creation of a pull request. It shows the typical messages and the URL provided for direct pull request creation.

```bash
posthog-website-contribution $ git push origin posthog-website-contribution
Total 0 (delta 0), reused 0 (delta 0), pack-reused 0
remote: 
remote: Create a pull request for 'posthog-website-contribution' on GitHub by visiting:
remote:      https://github.com/PostHog/posthog.com/pull/new/posthog-website-contribution
remote: 
To github.com:PostHog/posthog.com.git
* [new branch]        posthog-website-contribution -> posthog-website-contribution
```

--------------------------------

### Push Changes to GitHub via Command Line

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/handbook/engineering/posthog-com/developing-the-website.md

Details the process of pushing committed changes to a remote GitHub repository using the command line. It includes the push command syntax and an example, along with sample output indicating a new branch and the pull request creation link.

```bash
git push origin [branch-name]
```

```bash
git push origin posthog-website-contribution
```

```bash
posthog-website-contribution $ git push origin posthog-website-contribution
Total 0 (delta 0), reused 0 (delta 0), pack-reused 0
remote: 
remote: Create a pull request for 'posthog-website-contribution' on GitHub by visiting:
remote:      https://github.com/PostHog/posthog.com/pull/new/posthog-website-contribution
remote: 
To github.com:PostHog/posthog.com.git
* [new branch]        posthog-website-contribution -> posthog-website-contribution
```

--------------------------------

### Create a New Vue App using Vue CLI

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/tutorials/vue-analytics.md

Installs the Vue CLI globally and creates a new Vue 3 project. Ensure Node.js version 18.0 or newer is installed. Select 'Vue 3 (babel, eslint)' during project creation.

```bash
npm install -g @vue/cli
vue create vue-analytics
```

--------------------------------

### Docker Command to Run Caddy

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/docs/advanced/proxy/caddy.mdx

This bash command initiates a Caddy server using Docker. It maps ports 80 and 443, mounts a local Caddyfile for configuration, and uses a Docker volume for persistent data storage, which is crucial for Caddy's operation.

```bash
docker run -p 80:80 -p 443:443 \
  -v $PWD/Caddyfile:/etc/caddy/Caddyfile \
  -v caddy_data:/data \
  caddy
```

--------------------------------

### Create New Git Branch (Command Line)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/handbook/engineering/posthog-com/developing-the-website.md

This snippet demonstrates how to create a new Git branch using the command line. This is a standard Git operation used to isolate changes before merging them.

```bash
git checkout -b [new-branch-name]

# Example:
git checkout -b posthog-website-contribution
```

--------------------------------

### Clone and Start PostHog.com Development (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/README.md

Clones the PostHog.com repository and installs its dependencies using Yarn, then starts the development server. This is the primary command for local development. For Apple Silicon Macs, additional steps involving 'brew install vips' and removing 'node_modules' are required.

```bash
git clone git@github.com:PostHog/posthog.com.git && cd posthog.com/
rm -rf ./node_modules
brew install vips
yarn
yarn start
```

--------------------------------

### Install Gorilla Sessions Package

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/tutorials/go-analytics.md

This command installs the gorilla/sessions package, a popular Go library for managing user sessions using secure cookie-based storage. This is a prerequisite for handling user login state in the Go application.

```bash
go get github.com/gorilla/sessions
```

--------------------------------

### Install PostHog .NET SDK

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/docs/integrate/_snippets/install-dotnet.mdx

Installs the PostHog.AspNetCore package using the .NET CLI. This is the first step to integrating PostHog into your ASP.NET Core application.

```bash
dotnet add package PostHog.AspNetCore
```

--------------------------------

### Run End-to-End Tests with Cypress (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/handbook/engineering/developing-locally.md

Initiates the Cypress end-to-end test runner for PostHog. This command spins up a test instance and provides an interface for manually selecting and running tests. Requires `uv` to be installed.

```bash
bin/e2e-test-runner
```

--------------------------------

### Run Django Development Server and Migrations

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/tutorials/django-analytics.md

Commands to start the Django development server and apply database migrations for the initial project setup.

```bash
cd tutorial
python manage.py runserver
python manage.py migrate
```

--------------------------------

### Create Vue Project

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/tutorials/vue-cookie-banner.md

Creates a new Vue project named 'vue-cookie-banner' using the Vue CLI. It prompts the user to select Vue 3.

```bash
vue create vue-cookie-banner
```

--------------------------------

### Run Storybook Locally (Yarn)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/handbook/engineering/storybook.mdx

This command initiates the Storybook environment locally after the posthog.com repository has been cloned and dependencies installed. It opens a new browser window displaying the component library.

```bash
yarn storybook
```

--------------------------------

### Run Remix Development Server

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/tutorials/remix-analytics.md

Command to start the local development server for the Remix application. Navigate to the project directory first.

```bash
cd remix-tutorial
npm run dev
```

--------------------------------

### Clone Sample App and Install Dependencies

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/tutorials/llm-ab-tests.md

This bash script clones a sample Next.js application from GitHub and installs its Node.js dependencies. Ensure Node.js is installed before running.

```bash
git clone https://github.com/PostHog/llm-ab-tests-sample-app
cd llm-ab-tests-sample-app
npm install
```

--------------------------------

### Create Vue App and Install Dependencies

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/tutorials/vue-ab-tests.md

Installs the Vue CLI globally and creates a new Vue 3 project. This sets up the basic structure for the A/B testing application. Requires Node.js version 18.0 or newer.

```bash
npm install -g @vue/cli
vue create vue-ab-test
```

--------------------------------

### Install LiteLLM SDK or Proxy

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/docs/llm-analytics/installation/litellm.mdx

Provides commands to install the LiteLLM library for SDK usage or with proxy capabilities. For the proxy, Docker installation is also an option.

```bash
pip install litellm
```

```bash
pip install 'litellm[proxy]'

docker run --rm -p 4000:4000 ghcr.io/berriai/litellm:latest
```

--------------------------------

### Initialize Nuxt App Command

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/tutorials/nuxt-analytics.md

Command-line instruction to initialize a new Nuxt 3 project. It uses `npx` to run the `nuxi` CLI tool, creating a new project with specified configurations. This is the first step in setting up a Nuxt application.

```bash
npx nuxi@latest init <project-name>
```

--------------------------------

### Preview Production Build Locally (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/README.md

Builds the production version of the PostHog.com site and serves it locally. This command is useful for verifying how the site will appear in production before deployment.

```bash
gatsby build && gatsby serve
```

--------------------------------

### Create Next.js App with Bash

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/tutorials/anthropic-analytics.md

This command initializes a new Next.js application. Ensure Node.js version 18.0 or newer is installed. The prompts will guide you to configure TypeScript (select 'no'), the app router (select 'yes'), and other defaults.

```bash
npx create-next-app@latest claude-analytics
```

--------------------------------

### Upload Source Maps for PostHog Error Tracking

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/docs/error-tracking/installation/web.mdx

This example demonstrates uploading source maps to PostHog, which is crucial for de-minifying JavaScript errors. Source maps allow PostHog to display the original source code lines where errors occurred, significantly improving debugging. This is typically done via the PostHog CLI or API.

```bash
posthog-cli source-maps upload --url <your_app_url> --token <your_posthog_token> --directory <path_to_source_maps>
```

--------------------------------

### Create Next.js App (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/posthog/posthog.com/blob/master/contents/tutorials/intercom-session-replays.md

Scaffolds a new Next.js application using the `create-next-app` command-line tool. This command sets up a basic Next.js project structure with specified configurations, such as opting out of TypeScript and enabling the App Router.

```bash
npx create-next-app@latest intercom-sessions

```
